# Agent Runtime Patterns

## Summary

Reusable agent systems benefit from explicit capability models, centralized permission handling, deterministic tool-pool assembly, route-specific exploration docs, and separate summary lanes for durable notes, transcript compaction, and live progress.

Use [[AI Harness Engineering]] when the question is not just where runtime pieces live, but how context, tools, memory, permissions, orchestration, verification, observability, and repeated corrections work together as a model-adjacent control system.

## Tool Capability Model

Treat each tool as more than a name. Good runtime guidance usually needs:

- input schema
- whether it is a model tool or a user command surface
- permission behavior
- execution boundary
- read-only versus mutating behavior
- concurrency-safety signal
- user-facing render or output expectations

Keep user commands and model-invocable tools as separate surfaces even when they touch the same subsystems.

## Tool Pool Assembly

- keep one canonical assembly point for built-in, provider, and external tools
- merge deterministically
- filter visibility centrally instead of per feature screen
- avoid recreating overlapping tool lists in prompts, docs, and UI code

When the surface is large, prefer deferred discovery and honest search hints over dumping every tool definition into the primary prompt.

For agent-facing work, keep wiki routing, skill execution, app/plugin tools, and automations as separate capability classes. Use [[Capability Routing Standard]] to choose the smallest live capability set instead of loading every registry.

## Registry Discipline

- separate expensive discovery from live availability checks
- let auth, feature state, and connection state change availability without forcing a full reload of the registry
- keep command registries and model-tool registries distinct even if they share infrastructure
- keep static routing docs separate from the current session's live skill/tool inventory

## Permission Boundary

- permission policy should be centralized, not improvised inside each tool consumer
- modes should be explicit
- denials should preserve operator intent and avoid suggesting malicious bypasses
- if a workflow depends on permission assumptions, document them directly

Permission systems should be source-traceable and rule-based, not a pile of local exceptions.

Order matters:

- preserve hard denies first
- preserve content- or path-specific checks before convenience modes
- apply bypass or "don't ask" transforms last so earlier safety rules cannot be skipped accidentally

## MCP Lifecycle

- treat MCP as a lifecycle-managed subsystem
- make connection state explicit
- keep tool discovery and resource reads typed
- cache and invalidate deliberately instead of treating every lookup as stateless
- document client and server roles separately when the runtime supports both

## Exploration And Discoverability

Large agent runtimes need explicit orientation docs that answer questions like:

- where tools live
- where permissions are checked
- where memory is stored
- where MCP integration starts
- where sub-agent or coordinator behavior is owned

Good exploration docs reduce blind grep-first work.

Useful patterns include:

- architecture map
- subsystem map
- command and tool reference
- exploration guide with "start here for X" paths

## Memory Tiers

Use distinct memory jobs instead of one generic "memory" surface:

- working memory: the active context packet for the current task
- episodic memory: task history, logs, and run records
- semantic memory: durable maintained wiki pages and stable knowledge notes
- procedural memory: `AGENTS.md`, workflow pages, and skills

Where the runtime needs file-backed memory, scope it deliberately rather than treating memory as one opaque store.

## Active Goals

Treat Codex goals as a distinct continuity surface, not as ordinary memory, task plans, or reminders.

An active goal should carry:

- one persistent objective
- scope and source boundaries
- acceptance evidence
- next action and feedback signal
- explicit completion or blocked criteria

For agent-facing use, goal creation must stay an explicit action. Do not infer a persistent goal from every user request. Use [[Codex Goal Mechanism]] for the detailed operating contract.

## Memory Promotion And Recall

- memory promotion should be selective rather than transcript-wide
- store scope with each durable memory item such as user, agent, repo, run, or worktree
- filter by scope before ranking
- prefer additive history for mutable facts unless a stronger replacement rule is explicitly required
- keep memory recall packets small and ranked

## Summary Lanes

Keep these separate when the runtime supports them:

- durable session note for continuity
- transcript compaction summary for context reduction
- short progress summary for live UI or status

These artifacts have different cadence, persistence, and failure costs.

## Compaction Pattern

- compaction should create an explicit boundary
- when possible, compact from a durable note instead of resummarizing the entire transcript every time
- preserve a bounded recent suffix when continuity matters

## Feature-Gated Growth

- keep experimental subsystems behind explicit gates
- separate the gate from the default runtime path
- do not let coordinator, daemon, bridge, or remote features quietly become baseline behavior without deliberate routing and verification

## Startup Discipline

- prefetch only trust-safe or high-value state early
- defer noncritical work until after first render or first usable state
- lazy-load heavy or rare command paths
- use safe stubs when a gated subsystem is unavailable

## Agent UI Surfaces

- treat transcript state and tool state as first-class surfaces
- keep streaming and autoscroll behavior explicit
- render tool use with clear status rather than hiding it in raw text
- preserve accessibility affordances for live updates and focus movement

## Coding Agent Apps

For coding-agent systems or local agent shells, read [[Coding Agent Tooling Architecture]] before adding tool, MCP, memory, provider, or approval behavior. The reusable pattern is a small visible product surface backed by explicit runtime contracts for tools, permissions, context, evidence, evals, and traces.

## Rule

Do not let tool shape, memory shape, or permission shape remain implicit in a growing agent runtime.

If a recurring agent failure appears, decide which harness layer should change: route, source note, skill, tool contract, permission gate, memory rule, verification sensor, eval, or cleanup loop.
